steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage presents a study finding: Quito residents use parks far more than Fortaleza residents (82.9% vs. 35.7%), yet Quito residents reported much lower access to desired amenities near those parks. The question asks for the main idea — which means capturing both the core observation and the conclusion the passage explicitly draws from it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Even though the study found that parks in Fortaleza are more likely to be close to other amenities than parks in Quito are, Quito has more amenities overall than Fortaleza does.': Brings in "amenities overall" — the passage only discusses amenities near parks, never overall amenity counts. Fabricates a scope the passage doesn't address. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Although the study found that a greater proportion of residents use parks in Quito than in Fortaleza, that difference isn’t due to greater access to amenities near parks in Quito.': Names the observation (higher Quito park use) and the exact conclusion the passage states (that difference can't be explained by amenity access near parks). Matches the passage word for word in logic. ✓
  • Choice C — 'The study’s finding that a greater proportion of residents use parks in Quito than in Fortaleza is partly due to the greater prevalence of parks in Quito.': Attributes the difference to park prevalence — a factor the passage never mentions. Introduces a cause the study did not examine. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The study’s findings suggest that an increase in the number of amenities near city parks would likely increase park use in Quito but not in Fortaleza.': Speculates about what would happen if amenities increased — the passage makes no such prediction. Goes well beyond what the study found. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
